coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent turn-off/on bioassay for hemoglobin based on dual-emission carbon nanodots-graphene oxide system with multi-detection strategies
coredata.dc:creator: Qu, Fei
Liu, Dongya
You, Jinmao
coredata.dc:description: As two members of the carbon materials family, carbon nanodots (CNDs) and graphene oxide (GO) possess many excellent optical properties resulting in a wide range of applications. In this work, the fluorescence of resultant dual-emission carbon nanodots (DECNDs) could be quenched by GO. In the presence of hemoglobin (Hb), the fluorescence would recover resulting from two interactions: one was the direct stacking effect of Hb on GO; the other one was that Hb could cover the surfaces of DECNDs; both of them would prevent the fluorescence quenching of DECNDs by GO. In the light of this mechanism, a novel fluorescent turn-off/on method has been developed for the detection of Hb based on DECNDs-GO system. By virtue of the dual emissions of these CNDs, it is noteworthy that both a single emission and ratiometric of dual emissions can be used to establish linear relationships of Hb: 0.05–300 nM (λem = 386 nm), 5–500 nM (λem = 530 nm), and 50–500 nM (I530/I410), with the corresponding limit of detection (LOD) as low as 20 pM, 2 nM and 20 nM, respectively. This present system is highly selective toward Hb over other proteins and this reliable method has been successfully applied for the detection of Hb in whole blood samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Dual emissions
Graphene oxide
Hemoglobin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of Hb in whole blood samples
Fluorescence quenching effect of GO on DECNDs
Comparison of advantages and disadvantages of single emission measurement and ratiometric detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra of blue emitters (a) and yellow emitters (b) with addition of different volumes of GO; comparison of fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of blue emitters (c) and yellow emitters (d) with UV–vis absorption spectrum of GO (c, d). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure caption, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence spectra of blue emitters (BE, a) and yellow emitters (YE, b) in absence and presence of GO and Hb. The concentrations of probe, GO and Hb were 7.2 μg mL−1, 37 μg mL−1 and 200 nM for blue emitters, respectively; the concentrations of probe, GO and Hb were 64.8 μg mL−1, 68 μg mL−1 and 300 nM for yellow emitters, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure caption, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence spectra of DECNDs-GO system in the presence of different concentrations of Hb (a, blue emitters; b, yellow emitters) and corresponding linear ranges (inserts). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure caption, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Selectivity of DECNDs-GO system for the detection of Hb over other proteins, glucose and ascorbic acid (a, blue emitters; b, yellow emitters). For blue emitters, the concentration of Hb was 200 nM, and other proteins were 5 μM, and the concentrations of glucose and ascorbic acid were 20 μM; for yellow emitters, the concentration of Hb was 300 nM, and other proteins were 7.5 μM, and the concentrations of glucose and ascorbic acid were 30 μM. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure caption, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Detection of Hb based on the DECNDs-GO system.
Comparison of different optical methods for the determination of Hb.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of Hb in whole blood samples.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent turn-off/on bioassay for hemoglobin based on dual-emission carbon nanodots-graphene oxide system with multi-detection strategies

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Laboratory of Life-Organic Analysis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As two members of the carbon materials family, carbon nanodots (CNDs) and graphene oxide (GO) possess many excellent optical properties resulting in a wide range of applications. In this work, the fluorescence of resultant dual-emission carbon nanodots (DECNDs) could be quenched by GO. In the presence of hemoglobin (Hb), the fluorescence would recover resulting from two interactions: one was the direct stacking effect of Hb on GO; the other one was that Hb could cover the surfaces of DECNDs; both of them would prevent the fluorescence quenching of DECNDs by GO. In the light of this mechanism, a novel fluorescent turn-off/on method has been developed for the detection of Hb based on DECNDs-GO system. By virtue of the dual emissions of these CNDs, it is noteworthy that both a single emission and ratiometric of dual emissions can be used to establish linear relationships of Hb: 0.05–300 nM (λem = 386 nm), 5–500 nM (λem = 530 nm), and 50–500 nM (I530/I410), with the corresponding limit of detection (LOD) as low as 20 pM, 2 nM and 20 nM, respectively. This present system is highly selective toward Hb over other proteins and this reliable method has been successfully applied for the detection of Hb in whole blood samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Hemoglobin (Hb), a tetrameric metalloprotein, consists of a protein part (globin) and four iron-containing parts (heme). The existence of Hb in red blood cells is responsible for carrying oxygen from the lungs to various parts of the body through blood and carrying the main portion of carbon dioxide from these parts to the lungs [1,2]. The amount of Hb in blood is associated with many clinical diseases such as leukemia, anemia, heart diseases, etc. [3,4], while its normal level displays the well-functioning of the organism. Up to now, numerous analytical methods, including fluorometric [1,2,4,5], electrochemical [6,7], colorimetric [8] and chemiluminescence methods [9] have been reported for sensitive determination of Hb. However, some of these methods require expensive materials such as DNA aptamers, complicated probe preparation, or use of fluorescent dyes that suffer from photobleaching for sensitive detection of Hb [4]. Thus, there is a strong need for developing simple, reasonably accurate, and sensitive Hb measurement in human blood.

As new star members of the carbon family, graphene oxide (GO) and carbon nanodots (CNDs) have attracted tremendous attention, in which GO has been known as a superquencher of organic dyes, and CNDs are considered as a favorable candidate for the conventional heavy-metal-based quantum dots (QDs) because of their convenience in surface functionalization, low cytoxicity, good biocompatibility, and photostability [10–12]. In addition, some reports have studied the quenching effect of GO on CNDs [13–15] resulting from the energy transfer (ET) processes from photoexcited CNDs to the aromatic GO lattice [14] or the ultrafast electron transfer from CNDs to GO [15]. Utilizing the interaction between GO and CNDs, Qu et al. established protein sensors for recognition for ten proteins [16], however, in which the sensitivity of Hb was not satisfactory and the mechanism of detection was not discussed fully by the authors.

In our previous work, dual-emission carbon nanodots (DECNDs) were synthesized successfully in ethylene glycol-water binary systems, using ascorbic acid (AA) as carbon source. The dual emissions were ascribed to two types of CNDs due to the different modified ligands on their surfaces, such as AA capped on the blue emitters (λex = 315 nm, λem = 386 nm) and alcohol molecules capped on the yellow emitters (λex = 365 nm, λem = 530 nm) [17]. In this assay, we found that both fluorescence of these two emitters could be quenched effectively by GO. With addition of Hb, the dual emissions restored rapidly based on two reasons: (1) formation of GO-Hb composite via π–π stacking interaction [18], and (2) Hb capping on the surfaces of DECNDs. Both of them would prevent the fluorescence quenching of DECNDs by GO. Therefore, a simple, highly sensitive and selective fluorescent Hb sensor based on DECNDs-GO was developed without complicated, costly and time-consuming operations.

On the other hand, suffered from “false positive” of one emission-based probe due to multiple factors, such as the concentration of sensors, similar interferences, or environmental effects in complex samples, ratiometric fluorescent sensors were developed with high stability, anti-interference, and good reproducibility. In this paper, benefiting from the unique dual-emission property, both a single emission and ratiometric dual-emissions could be used as criteria for Hb detection, thus multi-detection strategies were available. For instance, for the blue emitters (λem = 386 nm), the linear range of Hb was 0.05–300 nM with limit of detection (LOD) 20 pM; for the yellow emitters (λem = 530 nm), the linear fashion was 5–500 nM with LOD 2 nM; taking advantage of intensity-ratiometric measurement of dual emissions (I530/I410), the linear scope was from 50 to 500 nM with LOD 20 nM. Besides, the present DECNDs-GO system exhibited high selectivity toward Hb over other proteins, such as myoglobin, bovine serum albumin (BSA), β-casein, transferrin, and cytochrome c. By virtue of the superior sensitivity and selectivity, this approach has been successfully applied for the detection of Hb in whole blood samples. To our best knowledge, this is the first time for the application of quenching interaction between DECNDs and GO. And this assay would inspire future investigations in developing dual-emission sensors and lead to new discoveries.

Ascorbic acid (AA), ethylene glycol, NaCl, hemoglobin (Hb), myoglobin, bovine serum albumin (BSA), β-casein, transferrin, cytochrome c, and glucose were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Natural graphite powder (325 mesh) was purchased from Qingdao Huatai lubricant sealing S&T Co. Ltd (Qingdao, China). Double distilled water was used for the preparation of all the solutions. All chemicals and solvents were obtained from the commercial sources and used without any further purification.

The fluorescence and resonant Rayleigh scattering spectra were collected with a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained on a Cary 300 Bio UV–vis spectrophotometer. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) measurements were obtained on a Tecnai G2 F20 electron microscope. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the samples were analyzed by a WQF-520A FTIR spectrophotometer using KBr pellets. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a Thermo Scientific/k-Alpha photoelectron spectrometer.

In this paper, the DECNDs were synthesized via one-pot hydrothermal method using AA as carbon source in ethylene glycol-water binary systems according to our previous report [17]. Typically, 0.8 g AA was dissolved in ethylene glycol-water reaction media. The total volume of the mixture was 20 mL and the volume fraction of ethylene glycol was 50%. Subsequently, under vigorous stirring to form a homogeneous solution, the mixture was heated to 160 °C in a constant temperature drying oven for 70 min. During this process, the color of the mixture changed from colorless to dark yellow, indicating the formation of DECNDs. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 10 min. Finally, a clear yellow aqueous dispersion containing DECNDs was obtained, and the DECNDs were stored at ambient environment. Using this method, the concentration of the synthesized DECNDs was ∼36 mg mL−1 and the mass yield of DECNDs reached ∼90%. Although the blue emitters and yellow emitters could be separated through chloroform extraction in our previous report [17], the DECNDs used to detect Hb were mixed emitters in this paper because chloroform was not suitable for bioassay.
Graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized by oxidation of graphite powder (3 g) according to the Hummers method [19]. The final concentration of GO was 0.33 mg mL−1.

At first, a suitable amount of Hb (5 μM) was added to the DECNDs solution (0.72 mg mL−1) with vigorous stirring. And then the GO solution (0.33 mg mL−1) was added to the mixture. After mixing to form a homogeneous solution, the signal output value was calculated according to the relative fluorescence intensity (FF0)/F0, in which F0 and F represented the fluorescence intensities of the DECNDs-GO system in the absence and presence of Hb, respectively. The optimization of experiment conditions was defined by relative fluorescence intensity after adding a moderate concentration of Hb into the sensor system. To study the linear relationship between the amount of Hb and (FF0)/F0, different concentrations of Hb were added to the assay solutions, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 315 and 365 nm, respectively. All data were the average values obtained by three parallel experiments at least in this paper.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Hb in whole blood samples
ce:section.ce:para: In brief, 10 μL of fingertip blood samples were collected from lab volunteers after cleaning their finger skin with alcohol and puncturing the skin deeply enough using a sharp needle. The collected blood samples were diluted to 1 mL with double-distilled water and then these solutions were subjected to erythrocyte lysis under sonication for 30 s. After lysis, the sample solutions were then centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 10 min. Subsequently, a moderate volume of supernatants was added to the DECNDs solution (0.72 mg mL−1) with vigorous stirring, and then the GO solution (0.33 mg mL−1) was added to the above mixture for fluorescence detection of Hb. Besides, a standard addition method was applied to evaluate the reliability of this method. For example, the blood samples with pretreatment were spiked with different concentrations of Hb (10, 30 and 50 nM, respectively), and then the mixtures were analyzed by DECNDs-GO system. All data were the average values obtained by three parallel experiments at least in this paper.

Recently, our group firstly synthesized dual-emission carbon nanodots (DECNDs), which contained two emitters: The blue emitters exhibited the maximum emission at 386 nm when excited at 315 nm, and yellow emitters showed a maximum in 530 nm with the excitation wavelength of 365 nm; the diameters of the DECNDs were mainly distributed in the range 1–3 nm with an average diameter of 1.81 nm; a variety of chemical functional groups were present on the surfaces of the DECNDs, such as CO, OH, CH, and COC, which were characterized by XPS spectra and FT-IR spectrum [17].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching effect of GO on DECNDs
ce:section.ce:para: The compositional similarity between GO and CNDs resulted in a better distribution of CNDs on GO sheets compared with other types of nanoparticles. In Fig. 1 a and b, GO could effectively quench the dual emissions and the quenching efficiency was proportional to the volumes of GO. In addition, the reaction could complete within 1 min, indicating the rapid reaction rate between DECNDs and GO. Some reports ascribed the fluorescence quenching of CNDs by GO to the energy transfer (ET) processes from photoexcited CNDs to the aromatic GO lattice [14] or the ultrafast electron transfer from CNDs to GO with a time constant of 400 fs [15], based on the hydrogen bonding [20]. However, in this assay, a quenching mechanism, inner filter effect (IFE), was proposed in view of the phenomena as follows (Fig. 1c and d): GO dispersion displayed a maximum absorption at 235 nm which was due to the π–π* transition of aromatic CC bonds, and a shoulder at 314 nm corresponding to the n–π* transition of the CO bond [21]; while the excitation wavelengths of DECNDs were 315 and 365 nm, respectively, the spectra showed quite precise overlap with GO absorption. As a consequence, the fluorescence quenching of DECNDs by GO was mainly attributed to IFE, which was known to result from the absorption of the exciting light by absorbers in the detection system with a highly efficient way [22–24]. Thus, GO could shield the excitation light, leading to the fluorescence quenching of DECNDs. Besides, as shown in Fig. 1c and d, the absorption spectrum of GO also displayed weak overlap with the emission spectra of DECNDs, so fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) was likely to be a partial reason of fluorescence quenching of DECNDs, which was validated by previous literature [25]. Hence, we believed that the quenching mechanism was attributed to combined effects of both IFE and FRET.

As shown in Fig. S1, upon addition of Hb, the absorption of GO showed a significant decrease on the basis of the interaction between GO and Hb. It had been well documented that the conjugate π-electron moieties of GO could combine with the aromatic groups of Hb, e.g., tryptophan/tyrosine residues and the porphyrin rings of the heme group via π–π stacking interaction [18]. Furthermore, there was an electrostatic attraction between positively charged Hb molecules and negatively charged GO sheets [26]. On the other hand, resonance Rayleigh scattering (RRS) spectra could also demonstrate the interaction between GO and Hb. As shown in Fig. S2, with addition of Hb, the RRS intensity increased sharply, supporting that the interaction between GO and Hb was responsible for the easy formation of GO-Hb composite.

By virtue of the interaction of GO and Hb mentioned above, the fluorescence recovery of DECNDs-GO system with addition of Hb was illustrated in Fig. 2 . For DECNDs, both of the dual emissions were nearly identical to those of the mixture of DECNDs and Hb (curve 1 and 2), indicating a negligible change with the addition of a suitable concentration of Hb; when GO was mixed with DECNDs, the fluorescence intensities decreased sharply within several seconds (curve 3), suggesting a high quenching rate; in the presence of Hb, the fluorescence of DECNDs–GO mixture enhanced obviously (curve 4), which revealed the interaction of GO-Hb was more stronger than that of DECNDs-GO. This process could also be observed under UV light at 365 nm (Fig. S3).

Additionally, the interaction between DECNDs and Hb was also investigated as shown in Fig. S4. The absorbance of two emitters enhanced obviously upon addition of Hb, displaying a direct interaction between DECNDs and Hb due to the hydrophobic effect [27]. However, previous literature reported that Hb could directly quench the fluorescence of CNDs due to IFE [4]. While in this assay, as shown in Fig. S5, a negligible fluorescence change was found when DECNDs were mixed with low concentrations of Hb (less than 500 nM) without GO. Therefore, based on the “switch” effect of GO, a sensitive and selective detection of Hb was developed. Hb molecules played two roles in this reaction (Scheme 1 ): one was direct stacking on GO substrates; the other one was capping on the surfaces of DECNDs. Both of them prevented the quenching effect of GO on DECNDs. Hence, a new strategy for the detection of Hb was developed based on a fluorescent turn-off/on mode by using DECNDs as probes and GO as a quencher.

Subsequently, the reaction conditions, such as the proper concentrations of blue emitters and yellow emitters, the amounts of GO, ionic strengths, reaction time, pH values and reagent addition orders, were also investigated in this assay (Figs. S6–S11).

The sensitivity of detection depended on the probe concentration. A lower concentration of probe achieved lower detection limits of Hb. Based on the different fluorescent properties of the two emitters, the optimal concentrations of DECNDs were 7.2 μg mL−1 for blue emitters and 64.8 μg mL−1 for yellow emitters, respectively (Fig. S6).

The fluorescence of DECNDs decreased linearly with the increasing volumes of GO, and when the concentrations of GO were 37 and 68 μg mL−1 respectively, the fluorescence quenching rates of two emissions were 55 and 51% (Fig. S7). However, excessive GO was disadvantage for the sensitive detection of Hb, so 37 μg mL−1 GO for blue emitters and 68 μg mL−1 GO for yellow emitters were chosen for the following experiments.

Moreover, the influence of ionic strength was carried out in NaCl solutions (0.1–900 mM), and there was no significant change in the detection of Hb based on DECNDs-GO system (Fig. S8), so such fluorescent turn-off/on sensors exhibited excellent stability under high ionic strength.

In the optimization of pH values, for DECNDs, the stable fluorescence was observed in the pH range 4–8 [17], thus the pH influence was investigated in this stable pH range and the optimal pH value was 6.0 (Fig. S9). Besides, the reactions could complete within 5 min (Fig. S10), realizing the fast determination of Hb.

Furthermore, the influence of addition order on the fluorescence of DECNDs was explored as shown in Fig. S11. Compared with the DECNDs-GO mixture, the fluorescence was enhanced upon addition of Hb. However, when Hb was pre-incubated with DECNDs, this order was able to produce the largest enhancement of fluorescence than other orders, which also suggested the two functions of Hb in this reaction: (1) direct stacking effect on GO; (2) capping on the surfaces of DECNDs. Therefore, the ideal assay procedure for Hb detection was the pre-incubation of DECNDs with Hb before adding GO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of advantages and disadvantages of single emission measurement and ratiometric detection
ce:section.ce:para: A single emission measurement possessed several advantages, such as convenience, easy operation and calculation. In this assay, when the approaches were proposed based on only blue emitters or yellow emitters, the single emission detection was more sensitive than the ratiometric measurement. Concretely, for detecting Hb blue emitters had a wider linear range and higher sensitivity than yellow emitters. Most importantly, in the detection of Hb, the two emitters had different emission wavelengths, which were 386 nm for blue emitters and 530 nm for yellow emitters, respectively. Hence, the blue or yellow emitters could be chosen as the optimal probe for application according to their different detection wavelengths in order to avoid the interference in the real samples.

However, the single emission measurement also had disadvantages that one emission-based probe would suffer from “false positive” and could be easily influenced by multiple factors, such as the concentration of sensors, similar interferences, or environmental effects in complex samples.

On the other hand, ratiometric measurement showed superior aspects, such as high stability, anti-interference, and good reproducibility. Especially, the ratiometric detection could be used as a calibration/reference method to estimate the “false positive” of one-emission probe in the application. In this paper, ratiometric detection displayed different limit of detection and linear range comparing with single emission measurement, and this would be feasible and flexible in the application. In addition, the synthesis of DECNDs proposed in this assay was easier and simpler comparing with that of other dual-emission nanomaterials [32–34].
The disadvantages of ratiometric measurement were also obvious: the sensitivity was not good enough, and the corresponding calculation was complex for detection of Hb.
In brief, this assay supplied multi-detection strategies, which could be employed flexibly and selectively according to the different samples and various environments.

The selectivity of the assay toward Hb over some possible interference was investigated, including myoglobin, bovine serum albumin (BSA), β-casein, transferrin, cytochrome c (Fig. 4 ). There was no interference from those proteins with concentrations 25 times greater than that of Hb. The excellent selectivity was related to the different isoelectronic points of these proteins, such as 10.8 for cytochrome c, 7.1 for Hb, 7.0 for myoglobin, 5.6 for transferrin, 5.0 for β-casein, and 4.7 for BSA [35]. In this assay, the optimal pH value was 6.0, while transferrin, β-casein and BSA possessed negative charges and difficulty accessed to the negatively charged surfaces of DECNDs due to electrostatic repulsion. Moreover, because of only one heme group in myoglobin and cytochrome c [4], their effect (direct stacking on GO substrates) would be weaker compared with Hb with four heme groups. Besides, 100-fold of glucose and ascorbic acid also had a negligible influence on the detection of Hb. Therefore, the two types of CNDs exhibited an excellent selectivity to Hb against other interfering substances.

In order to evaluate the feasibility of the proposed method, the DECNDs-GO system was used to detect Hb in human whole blood samples. In Table 2 , the found amount was the Hb concentration in the blood sample without addition of standard, while the found values were the total concentrations of Hb with addition of standard. The recoveries of Hb in spiked human blood were 95–98%. Hence, the method was validated to provide enough selectivity, sensitivity and stability in determination of Hb in clinical diagnostics, which is essential to assess the extent of anemia.

In this study, we combined the special optical properties of DECNDs with super quenching ability of GO to develop a fluorescent turn-off/on biosensor for Hb detection. The dual emissions of DECNDs could be quenched effectively by GO via IFE and FRET, while with addition of Hb, the fluorescence of this analytical system restored rapidly. Moreover, both single emission and ratiometric dual-emissions could be used to construct linear calibration curves for Hb, and the multi-detection strategies ensured the reliability, accuracy, and good selectivity of this sensing system. On the basis of superior sensitivity and selectivity, this protocol was applied to detect Hb in human whole blood samples, and satisfactory recoveries were obtained. Therefore, the DECNDs-GO system was a promising candidate for Hb detection in clinic without complicated modifications and expensive instruments, which was also essential to assess the extent of anemia and to evaluate various intervention programs aiming at control of anemia.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Fei Qu and Dongya Liu contributed equally to this work.
This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Youth Fund Project) (21405093) and the Scientific Research Foundation of Qufu Normal University (BSQD20130117).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2016.03.048.

Graphene oxide membranes with tunable permeability due to embedded carbon dots

Plugging into proteins: poisoning protein function by a hydrophobic nanoparticle
